title: Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena: Implications on National Security, Public Safety, and Government Transparency
location: 2154 Rayburn House Office Building, Washington, D.C.
subcommittee: Subcommittee on National Security, the Border, and Foreign Affairs
summary: A public House hearing examining military and commercial aviation encounters with unidentified anomalous phenomena, federal handling and classification of UAP information, whistleblower protections, public safety, national security, and congressional oversight.
editorialNote: GreyAlien distinguishes established hearing facts from allegations and witness claims. Inclusion here does not mean every claim has been independently verified.

witnesses.name: Ryan Graves
witnesses.roleAtHearing: Executive Director, Americans for Safe Aerospace
witnesses.testimonySummary: Graves focused on recurring military and commercial pilot reports, aviation-safety risks, stigma, reporting gaps, classification, and the need for an empowered and transparent investigative process.
keyPoints: He testified that military and commercial aircrews report UAP encounters with regularity.
He described stigma and professional consequences as barriers to reporting.
He called for accurate reporting, independent investigation, and greater public transparency.

witnesses.name: Commander David Fravor (Ret.)
witnesses.roleAtHearing: Former Commanding Officer, United States Navy
witnesses.background: Retired U.S. Navy aviator and former commanding officer of Strike Fighter Squadron 41
witnesses.testimonySummary: Fravor described the November 2004 USS Nimitz 'Tic Tac' encounter and argued that reported government programs involving anomalous craft or technology should be subject to congressional oversight.
keyPoints: He recounted a 2004 visual encounter involving Navy aircrews and radar tracking.
He described the object's apparent performance as beyond the aircraft available to him at the time.
He emphasized oversight and accountability for any government program involving such technology.

witnesses.name: David Grusch
witnesses.roleAtHearing: Former National Reconnaissance Office Representative, UAP Task Force, Department of Defense
witnesses.background: Former U.S. Air Force intelligence officer and former National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency employee
witnesses.claimStatus: Allegations presented under oath; not independently established by this page
witnesses.testimonySummary: Grusch testified about information he said was provided by current and former officials concerning alleged long-running crash-retrieval and reverse-engineering activities, access restrictions, retaliation, and lack of congressional oversight.
keyPoints: He said his public statements were based largely on information supplied by other individuals rather than personal observation of recovered craft.
He alleged that relevant programs had operated without proper congressional oversight.
He said he had submitted information through inspector-general and whistleblower channels.

timeline.label: Hearing first published in the House Committee Repository
Committee announced the hearing and its purpose
Public hearing held at 10:00 a.m. in 2154 Rayburn
Official transcript added to the House Committee Repository

documents.label: Official hearing page
documents.sourceType: House Committee on Oversight and Accountability

whyItMatters: This hearing moved the modern UAP debate from agency officials to firsthand witnesses and a former intelligence officer. It expanded congressional attention from reporting systems and flight safety to whistleblower protection, alleged hidden programs, classification, and the scope of congressional access.

evidenceProfile.summary: The hearing, transcript, witness statements, and committee documents are official. Individual witness claims vary in evidentiary status.
